title: Container ignores Ctrl-C unless explicitly trapped
body:
### Issue Description

There are a bunch of GH issues to be found related to Ctrl-C handling, and if you dig through them all, you might come across the solution below, but I believe there's still a bug to be fixed in Podman itself.

I've boiled it down to this: a program that will die when you hit Ctrl-C when you run it out on the host won't die when run under podman *unless* it explicitly traps `SIGINT`. The question is, why is `SIG_DFL` not doing the same thing on both sides of that barrier?

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. [Unpack the test](https://github.com/containers/podman/files/11245052/test.zip) in a temp directory somewhere, then say "`make run`".
2. If you wait it out, it exits in 5 seconds, but if you try accelerating its inevitable doom with a Ctrl-C, it ignores you.
3. Uncomment the line in `test.c`, then say `make run` again. Now you can kill it with Ctrl-C. Why? If you say "`make test ; ./test`" to replicate the first step out on the host, it *does* die with Ctrl-C, even with that `signal()` call still commented out in `test.c`. Why the difference?

### Describe the results you received

The test program doesn't die until you uncomment the `signal()` call.

### Describe the results you expected

Ctrl-C should kill the program even with `signal(SIGINT, SIG_DFL)`, as it does out on the host, without Podman in the way.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

I've tested this both on EL9 using the version of Podman it comes with (4.4.1) and on macOS 13 with the latest Podman 4.5.0 from Homebrew.

comments.body:
I should add that if you change all the things necessary to get the test code linked above to work under Docker, you get the same behavior.

However, this is one of the areas where Podman can show itself superior by not emulating bad decisions.

that is not bad handling by Docker, it is just the different semantic for "PID 1" where the default action for signals is to ignore them.

You either need to change the program, as you did, to explicitly handle the signals or you need to run with another pid using an init program: you can achieve it with `podman run --rm -it --init ...`
Duh, I forget these details. Sorry for the noise.
